In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib_scalebar.scalebar import ScaleBar
import numpy as np
import requests
import geopandas as gpd
from shapely.geometry import Point
import folium
from esda import moran
import libpysal

The Green Effect: Investigating the Green Gentrification in East London

Context and Problem

This essay describes the effect of the "Green Paradox"; environmental gentrification caused by "Green Projects", including but not limited to: park restorations, wetland projects, or "blandscaping". Urban Greening is often described as a "universal win" for the public and climate, however, this "Green Paradox" inadvertently drives up property values, displacing lower-income and long-term residents (Gould & Lewis, 2017). Scholars have often argued for a "just green enough" strategy to mitigate these effects, yet the pressure of ecological attraction remains a significant driver of urban displacement.

One project which comes to mind is the Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park. Consequently, East London house prices rose ~64% between 2012 and 2017 (Halifax, 2017), outperforming the rest of the capital. Additionally, house prices in Waltham Forest grew 122% in the 10 years since the Olympics, representing the ever-growing rent gap coined not by industrial development but by ecological aestheticization (Smith, 1987). As outlined by Lloyds Bank (2022), these shifts highlight the complex intersection of environmental planning and social equity, where sustainability initiatives can inadvertently become tools of exclusion.

As such, my research explores the relationship between green space density and socio-economic shift in and around London. By utilising Geospatial tools, I will visualise the distribution of parks (via OpenStreetMap Overpass API) against indices of multiple deprivation (IMD) and housing costs. This technical approach allows for a granular analysis of how "green lungs" in a city can also drive displacement through a process of ecological exclusion (Wolch et al., 2014; Immergluck & Balan, 2018; James, 2005). Ultimately, the map aims to provide a critical lens on the "Green Paradox" within the context of London’s evolving urban landscape.

In [6]:
# 3. CLEANING: Ensure the 'Borough Name' column looks the same in both
# (e.g. both are "Hackney" not "hackney " or "London Borough of Hackney")
# Strip whitespace and convert to title case
land_house_prices['Area'] = land_house_prices['Area'].str.strip().str.title()
london_gdf['NAME'] = london_gdf['NAME'].str.strip().str.title()

# Convert Value to numeric (As it contained commas, remove commas)
land_house_prices['Value'] = land_house_prices['Value'].astype(str).str.replace(',', '').astype(float)

# Filter for 'Mean' measure and calculate the average price across all years for each borough
mean_prices = land_house_prices[land_house_prices['Measure'] == 'Mean']
avg_prices_by_borough = mean_prices.groupby('Area')['Value'].mean().reset_index()
avg_prices_by_borough = avg_prices_by_borough.rename(columns={'Value': 'AveragePrice'})
In [7]:
#Merge the two dataframes on the borough name
merged_gdf = london_gdf.merge(avg_prices_by_borough, left_on='NAME', right_on='Area')
print(merged_gdf.columns)

       'SUB_2006', 'geometry', 'Area', 'AveragePrice'],
      dtype='object')
In [8]:
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker
from matplotlib_scalebar.scalebar import ScaleBar

# Calculate quantiles to tighten the color ramp (removes extreme outliers from color scale)
vmin_price = merged_gdf['AveragePrice'].quantile(0.05)
vmax_price = merged_gdf['AveragePrice'].quantile(0.95)

# Plot the average house price by borough
ax = merged_gdf.plot(
    column='AveragePrice', 
    cmap='OrRd', 
    legend=True,
    vmin=vmin_price,
    vmax=vmax_price,
    legend_kwds={'format': ticker.StrMethodFormatter('£{x:,.0f}')} # Format with commas and £, removes 1e6
)

# Add Scalebar (assuming CRS is EPSG:27700 where 1 unit = 1 meter)
scalebar = ScaleBar(1, units='m', location='lower right')
ax.add_artist(scalebar)

# Add North Arrow
ax.annotate('N', xy=(0.05, 0.95), xytext=(0.05, 0.85),
            arrowprops=dict(facecolor='black', width=4, headwidth=10),
            ha='center', va='center', fontsize=15, xycoords='axes fraction')

ax.set_axis_off() # Remove the x and y axis
plt.title('Map 1: Average House Price by London Borough')
plt.show()

In [10]:
#Clean the IMD data, so that the borough names match those in the geodataframe
imd_data['Local Authority District name (2019)'] = imd_data['Local Authority District name (2019)'].str.strip().str.title()
london_gdf['NAME'] = london_gdf['NAME'].str.strip().str.title()

# IMD data is usually at the LSOA (neighborhood) level, meaning there are multiple rows per borough.
# We need to group by the borough name and calculate the average IMD score before merging.
borough_imd = imd_data.groupby('Local Authority District name (2019)')['Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) Score'].mean().reset_index()

#Perform a merge on borough name with the aggregated IMD data
merged_gdf = london_gdf.merge(borough_imd, left_on='NAME', right_on='Local Authority District name (2019)')
print(merged_gdf.columns)

Index(['NAME', 'GSS_CODE', 'HECTARES', 'NONLD_AREA', 'ONS_INNER', 'SUB_2009',
       'SUB_2006', 'geometry', 'Local Authority District name (2019)',
       'Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) Score'],
      dtype='object')

Map Analysis

There is a clear contrast of the two static maps above which establishes the groundwork for the "Green Paradox" in London. Map 1 shows that property wealth is historically & heavily concentrated in Central & West London (e.g., Kensington, Westminster). Conversely, Map 2 also shows a stark spatial dichotomy, with the highest levels of structural vulnerability (dark red IMD scores) clustered in the East London boroughs, specifically Newham, Hackney, Tower Hamlets and Barking & Dagenham.

In geographic terms, this inverse relationship via spatial autocorrelation between high deprivation and low initial property values represents a massive "rent gap" (Smith, 1987). These eastern boroughs (historically underinvested and vulnerable) became "ground zero" for the "green projects" i.e. 2012 Olympic regeneration. When aggressive "green" infrastructure is injected into such areas, consequently triggering rapid price growth. The vulnerable populations highlighted in Map 2 are therefore the most at risk of ecological displacement as the relatively cheaper housing in Map 1 becomes premium, "green-adjacent" real estate.

Presenting and Motivating Data Sources

1. Socio-Economic Vulnerability (IMD 2019 Data):
To understand who is at risk of displacement, I used the Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) metric at the Local Authority District level. Rather than relying solely on income, IMD provides a robust, composite measure of structural vulnerability, encompassing health, education, and living environment in one. This dataset is crucial for identifying the historically marginalised communities in East London that are most susceptible to ecological exclusion.

2. Economic Displacement (UK House Price / Land Registry Data):
To quantify the economic reality of gentrification, I incorporated average house price data. By mapping the cost of housing alongside IMD, we can visualise the "rent gap." This data acts as the ultimate proxy for displacement pressure, highlighting where the cost of living is rapidly outpacing the socioeconomic capacity of the original residents.

3. Environmental Interventions (OpenStreetMap API):
As government data is often temporally lagged, measuring green gentrification requires dynamic, real-time data. As such, I will utilise the OpenStreetMap (OSM) Overpass API to map the "Green" interventions. OSM provides highly granular vector data, capturing everything from the massive Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park down to localised "pocket parks."

Together, the IMD dataset in tandem with UK house price data and the OpenStreetMap API allows us to test the spatial correlation between the injection of new green projects (OSM) and the capturing of economic pressures (House Prices) on vulnerable populations (IMD).

API and Backend Request

In [12]:
# Multiple public Overpass API mirrors — tried in order if one times out
OVERPASS_ENDPOINTS = [
    "https://overpass.kumi.systems/api/interpreter",
    "https://maps.mail.ru/osm/tools/overpass/api/interpreter",
    "http://overpass-api.de/api/interpreter",
]

def get_london_parks(Borough) -> gpd.GeoDataFrame:
    """
    Fetches the realtime park data for a given London borough using the Overpass API.
    Step 1: Nominatim resolves the borough name → correct OSM relation ID by finding
            the administrative boundary (class=boundary, type=administrative).
    Step 2: Overpass uses that area ID to fetch all parks within the borough boundary.
            Falls back through multiple Overpass mirrors if one times out.
    """
    headers = {'User-Agent': 'LondonGreenGentrificationStudy/1.0'}

    # ── Step 1: Resolve the borough's OSM relation via Nominatim ──────────────
    nominatim_url = "https://nominatim.openstreetmap.org/search"
    nom_params = {
        'q': f'{Borough}, London, United Kingdom',
        'format': 'json',
        'limit': 10,
        'addressdetails': 1,
    }

    try:
        nom_resp = requests.get(nominatim_url, params=nom_params, headers=headers, timeout=15)
        nom_resp.raise_for_status()
        nom_data = nom_resp.json()

        if not nom_data:
            print(f"Nominatim could not find borough: '{Borough}'")
            return gpd.GeoDataFrame(columns=['name', 'geometry'])

        # Pick the first result that is an administrative boundary relation
        boundary = next(
            (r for r in nom_data
             if r.get('osm_type') == 'relation'
             and r.get('class') == 'boundary'
             and r.get('type') == 'administrative'),
            None
        )

        if boundary is None:
            print(f"No administrative boundary relation found for '{Borough}'.")
            return gpd.GeoDataFrame(columns=['name', 'geometry'])

        osm_id  = int(boundary['osm_id'])
        area_id = osm_id + 3_600_000_000   # Overpass area ID for a relation
        print(f"Nominatim resolved '{Borough}' → OSM relation {osm_id} (area id: {area_id})")

    except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
        print(f"Nominatim error: {e}")
        return gpd.GeoDataFrame(columns=['name', 'geometry'])

    # ── Step 2: Fetch parks from Overpass (try each mirror in turn) ────────────
    query = f"""
    [out:json][timeout:90];
    area(id:{area_id})->.searchArea;
    (
      node["leisure"="park"](area.searchArea);
      way["leisure"="park"](area.searchArea);
      relation["leisure"="park"](area.searchArea);
    );
    out center;
    """

    data = None
    for endpoint in OVERPASS_ENDPOINTS:
        try:
            print(f"Trying Overpass endpoint: {endpoint}")
            response = requests.get(endpoint, params={'data': query}, timeout=60)
            response.raise_for_status()
            data = response.json()
            print(f"Success with: {endpoint}")
            break
        except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
            print(f"  → failed ({e.__class__.__name__}), trying next...")

    if data is None:
        print("All Overpass endpoints failed.")
        return gpd.GeoDataFrame(columns=['name', 'geometry'])

    elements = data.get('elements', [])
    print(f"Overpass returned {len(elements)} park elements for '{Borough}'")

    parks_list = []
    for element in elements:
        name = element.get('tags', {}).get('name', 'Unnamed Park')

        if element['type'] == 'node':
            lat = element.get('lat')
            lon = element.get('lon')
        elif element['type'] in ['way', 'relation']:
            center = element.get('center', {})
            lat = center.get('lat')
            lon = center.get('lon')
        else:
            continue

        if lat is not None and lon is not None:
            parks_list.append({'name': name, 'geometry': Point(lon, lat)})

    if not parks_list:
        print(f"No parks found for borough: {Borough}")
        return gpd.GeoDataFrame(columns=['name', 'geometry'])

    parks_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(parks_list, geometry='geometry')
    parks_gdf.set_crs(epsg=4326, inplace=True)
    return parks_gdf
In [16]:
# Example usage: List of boroughs to investigate for Green Gentrification
borough_names = ["Hackney", "Kensington and Chelsea", "Tower Hamlets", "Newham", "Waltham Forest"]
parks_dict = {}

for b in borough_names:
    print(f"Fetching parks for {b}...")
    gdf = get_london_parks(b)
    if gdf is not None and not gdf.empty:
        parks_dict[b] = gdf
        print(f'Total parks fetched for {b}: {len(gdf)}')

API Call (A1): How the parks data is fetched

An API (Application Programming Interface) is a structured way for one program to request data from another. In this project, I utilised the OpenStreetMap library (Overpass API) to query live geospatial data tagged as leisure=park. The program then sends an HTTP request containing an Overpass query, and the server returns a JSON response of matching OSM elements (nodes, ways, and relations). Because many parks are mapped as polygons (ways/relations), the query uses out center; instead of some out node so each park returns it's centroid coordinate's.

And so to ensure I fetch the correct Hackney (and not places with the same name elsewhere), I first use Nominatim (OSM’s geocoding service) to resolve “Hackney, London” to the borough’s administrative boundary relation. That relation ID is then converted to an Overpass area id, thus making our subsequent park query much more precise. Finally, the JSON is parsed into a GeoPandas GeoDataFrame (with a proper CRS), which can be plotted and passed directly into Folium.

This pipeline ensures the interactive map is possible because the map is driven by on-demand API calls: changing borough inputs triggers new requests, new geometries, and an updated visual layer, linking the narrative about green projects to a fully working, data-backed interactive display.

Interactive Map Implementation

To make the map more modular and contextually relevant, I dynamically filtered the london_gdf GeoDataFrame to extract the specific boundary of the queried borough (e.g., Hackney). Rather than loading the entire London shapefile into Folium—which would be computationally heavy and visually distracting—I isolated the single borough polygon and added it as a folium.GeoJson layer. This approach ensures that the map automatically highlights the correct administrative boundary corresponding to the API request, providing a clear spatial container for the clustered park data.

In [18]:
import folium
from folium.plugins import MarkerCluster

# Build an interactive map from the parks GeoDataFrame.
if not parks_dict:
    print("No park data available to map. Run the API cell first.")
else:
    # 1) Find a sensible centre (mean of all park points)
    all_lats = []
    all_lons = []
    for gdf in parks_dict.values():
        all_lats.extend(gdf.geometry.y.tolist())
        all_lons.extend(gdf.geometry.x.tolist())
        
    center_lat = sum(all_lats) / len(all_lats)
    center_lon = sum(all_lons) / len(all_lons)

    park_map = folium.Map(location=[center_lat, center_lon], zoom_start=11, tiles="CartoDB positron")
    
    # Define a color palette for the boroughs
    borough_colors = {
        "Hackney": "#2E7D32", # Dark Green
        "Kensington and Chelsea": "#1565C0", # Blue
        "Tower Hamlets": "#C62828", # Red
        "Newham": "#6A1B9A", # Purple
        "Waltham Forest": "#EF6C00" # Orange
    }
    
    # Define a custom icon create function for the MarkerCluster
    # This overrides the default Leaflet cluster icon to show "Parks: {count}"
    # and adjusts the size to fit the text.
    icon_create_function = """
    function(cluster) {
        var childCount = cluster.getChildCount();
        var c = ' marker-cluster-';
        if (childCount < 10) {
            c += 'small';
        } else if (childCount < 100) {
            c += 'medium';
        } else {
            c += 'large';
        }
        return new L.DivIcon({
            html: '<div style="width: 70px; height: 30px; margin-left: 5px; margin-top: 5px; border-radius: 15px; display: flex; justify-content: center; align-items: center;"><span style="line-height: normal; font-size: 12px; font-weight: bold;">Parks: ' + childCount + '</span></div>',
            className: 'marker-cluster' + c,
            iconSize: new L.Point(80, 40)
        });
    }
    """
    
    # --- Add the Borough Boundaries and Parks ---
    for b_name, gdf in parks_dict.items():
        color = borough_colors.get(b_name, "gray")
        
        # Filter the london_gdf to only include the selected borough (case-insensitive to handle "And" vs "and")
        borough_boundary = london_gdf[london_gdf['NAME'].str.lower() == b_name.lower()]
        
        # Create a FeatureGroup for this borough to allow toggling
        borough_layer = folium.FeatureGroup(name=f"{b_name} Parks & Boundary")
        
        if not borough_boundary.empty:
            folium.GeoJson(
                borough_boundary,
                name=f"{b_name} Boundary",
                style_function=lambda x, c=color: {'fillColor': c, 'color': c, 'weight': 2, 'fillOpacity': 0.1}
            ).add_to(borough_layer)
        else:
            print(f"Warning: Boundary not found for {b_name}")
            
        # Add parks to a MarkerCluster within the FeatureGroup
        cluster = MarkerCluster(
            name=f"{b_name} Parks",
            icon_create_function=icon_create_function
        ).add_to(borough_layer)

        for _, row in gdf.iterrows():
            folium.CircleMarker(
                location=[row.geometry.y, row.geometry.x],
                radius=4,
                color=color,
                fill=True,
                fill_color=color,
                fill_opacity=0.8,
                popup=f"Parks: {len(gdf)} in {b_name}",
                tooltip=f"{row.get('name', 'Unnamed Park')} ({b_name})"
            ).add_to(cluster)
            
        borough_layer.add_to(park_map)

    # Add a choropleth layer for the IMD scores by borough
    # We use the selected boroughs for the choropleth to keep it focused (case-insensitive)
    lower_borough_names = [b.lower() for b in borough_names]
    selected_boundaries = london_gdf[london_gdf['NAME'].str.lower().isin(lower_borough_names)]
    
    if not selected_boundaries.empty:
        folium.Choropleth(
            geo_data=selected_boundaries, #GeoJSON for the borough boundaries
            name='IMD Score (Selected Boroughs)', #Name for the layer control
            data=merged_gdf[merged_gdf['NAME'].str.lower().isin(lower_borough_names)], #Dataframe containing the IMD scores
            columns=['NAME', 'Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) Score'],
            key_on='feature.properties.NAME',
            fill_color='RdYlGn_r',
            fill_opacity=0.6,
            line_opacity=0.8,
            legend_name='Average IMD Score by Borough'
        ).add_to(park_map)

    folium.LayerControl().add_to(park_map)

    # Fit bounds dynamically so the map zooms perfectly to the data limits
    if all_lats and all_lons:
        min_lat, max_lat = min(all_lats), max(all_lats)
        min_lon, max_lon = min(all_lons), max(all_lons)
        park_map.fit_bounds([[min_lat, min_lon], [max_lat, max_lon]])

    park_map.save("selected_boroughs_parks_map.html")

Spatial Autocorrelation

In [17]:
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd

# 1. Combine all fetched parks into a single GeoDataFrame
if 'parks_dict' in locals() and parks_dict:
    all_parks_gdf = pd.concat(parks_dict.values(), ignore_index=True)
    
    # 2. Load LSOA shapefile
    lsoa_shp_path = r"statistical-gis-boundaries-london\statistical-gis-boundaries-london\ESRI\LSOA_2011_London_gen_MHW.shp"
    lsoa_gdf = gpd.read_file(lsoa_shp_path)


    # 4. Merge LSOA shapefile with IMD data
    lsoa_imd_gdf = lsoa_gdf.merge(imd_data, left_on='LSOA11CD', right_on='LSOA code (2011)', how='inner')
    
    # 5. Ensure CRS matches before spatial join
    if all_parks_gdf.crs != lsoa_imd_gdf.crs:
        all_parks_gdf = all_parks_gdf.to_crs(lsoa_imd_gdf.crs)
    
    # 6. Perform Spatial Join (parks within LSOAs)
    # We use 'intersects' to capture parks that fall within or touch an LSOA
    parks_with_imd = gpd.sjoin(all_parks_gdf, lsoa_imd_gdf, how="inner", predicate="intersects")
    
    # 7. Calculate Statistics
    total_parks = len(parks_with_imd)
    
    # "40% most deprived LSOAs" means IMD Decile 1, 2, 3, 4
    decile_col = 'Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) Decile (where 1 is most deprived 10% of LSOAs)'
    deprived_parks = parks_with_imd[parks_with_imd[decile_col] <= 4]
    percent_in_deprived = (len(deprived_parks) / total_parks) * 100 if total_parks > 0 else 0
    
    # Tower Hamlets stats
    borough_col = 'Local Authority District name (2019)'
    tower_hamlets_parks = parks_with_imd[parks_with_imd[borough_col] == 'Tower Hamlets']
    percent_in_th = (len(tower_hamlets_parks) / total_parks) * 100 if total_parks > 0 else 0
    
    print(f"Total mapped parks (after spatial join): {total_parks}")
    print(f"Parks in 40% most deprived LSOAs: {len(deprived_parks)} ({percent_in_deprived:.1f}%)")
    print(f"Parks in Tower Hamlets: {len(tower_hamlets_parks)} ({percent_in_th:.1f}%)")
else:
    print("No parks data available to perform spatial join. Please run the API cells first.")

Total mapped parks (after spatial join): 534
Parks in 40% most deprived LSOAs: 412 (77.2%)
Parks in Tower Hamlets: 229 (42.9%)
In [19]:
import libpysal
import esda
from splot.esda import lisa_cluster
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

# 1. Calculate Park Density per LSOA
# Count the number of parks in each LSOA from our spatial join
park_counts = parks_with_imd.groupby('LSOA11CD').size().reset_index(name='park_count')

# Merge the counts back to the LSOA + IMD GeoDataFrame
lsoa_analysis_gdf = lsoa_imd_gdf.merge(park_counts, on='LSOA11CD', how='left')

# Fill NaNs with 0 (LSOAs with no parks)
lsoa_analysis_gdf['park_count'] = lsoa_analysis_gdf['park_count'].fillna(0)

# Filter to only include the boroughs we fetched park data for
target_boroughs = ["Hackney", "Kensington and Chelsea", "Tower Hamlets", "Newham", "Waltham Forest"]
lsoa_analysis_gdf = lsoa_analysis_gdf[lsoa_analysis_gdf['Local Authority District name (2019)'].isin(target_boroughs)].copy()

# Reset index to ensure weights matrix aligns perfectly
lsoa_analysis_gdf = lsoa_analysis_gdf.reset_index(drop=True)

# Calculate park density (parks per hectare)
# The CRS is EPSG:27700 (meters), so area is in square meters. 1 hectare = 10,000 sq meters.
lsoa_analysis_gdf['park_density'] = lsoa_analysis_gdf['park_count'] / (lsoa_analysis_gdf.geometry.area / 10000)

# 2. Spatial Weights Matrix
# Using K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) instead of Queen contiguity to avoid "islands" (disconnected polygons)
# k=8 is a standard choice for polygon neighborhoods
w = libpysal.weights.KNN.from_dataframe(lsoa_analysis_gdf, k=8)
w.transform = 'r' # Row-standardize the weights

# 3. Bivariate Spatial Autocorrelation
# Variables: IMD Score (Deprivation) and Park Density
x = lsoa_analysis_gdf['Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) Score'].values
y = lsoa_analysis_gdf['park_density'].values

# Calculate Global Bivariate Moran's I
# This measures the correlation between a location's IMD and its neighbors' Park Density
moran_bv = esda.Moran_BV(x, y, w)
print(f"Global Bivariate Moran's I: {moran_bv.I:.3f}")
print(f"P-value: {moran_bv.p_sim:.3f}")

# Calculate Local Bivariate Moran's I (LISA)
lisa_bv = esda.Moran_Local_BV(x, y, w)

# 4. Plot LISA Map
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 12))
# Disable default legend to use manual matplotlib.patches legend
plt_fig, plt_ax = lisa_cluster(lisa_bv, lsoa_analysis_gdf, p=0.05, ax=ax, legend=False)

# Add Borough Boundaries and Labels for context
# Filter the main london_gdf for our target boroughs
borough_boundaries = london_gdf[london_gdf['NAME'].isin(target_boroughs)]

# Plot the borough outlines over the LISA map
borough_boundaries.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color='black', linewidth=1, alpha=0.5)

# Add text labels for the boroughs
for idx, row in borough_boundaries.iterrows():
    # Get the centroid of the borough for label placement
    centroid = row.geometry.centroid
    ax.annotate(text=row['NAME'], 
                xy=(centroid.x, centroid.y), 
                xytext=(3, 3), 
                textcoords="offset points",
                fontsize=10, 
                fontweight='bold', 
                color='black',
                ha='center',
                bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.3", fc="white", ec="gray", alpha=0.7))

# Create custom manual legend using matplotlib.patches
legend_elements = [
    mpatches.Patch(color='#d7191c', label='High IMD - High Park Density (HH)\n(Deprived but Green)'),
    mpatches.Patch(color='#2c7bb6', label='Low IMD - Low Park Density (LL)\n(Wealthy but Sparse)'),
    mpatches.Patch(color='#abd9e9', label='Low IMD - High Park Density (LH)\n(Wealthy and Green)'),
    mpatches.Patch(color='#fdae61', label='High IMD - Low Park Density (HL)\n(Deprived and Sparse)'),
    mpatches.Patch(color='#eeeeee', label='Not Significant (ns)')
]

# Add legend to axis
ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), title='LISA Clusters', fontsize=10, title_fontsize=12)

ax.set_title('Bivariate LISA Cluster Map: IMD Score vs. Spatial Lag of Park Density\n(Selected Boroughs)', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Global Bivariate Moran's I: -0.005
P-value: 0.421

Spatial Autocorrelation Analysis

Employing Global Bivariate Moran's I yielded a near-zero, statistically insignificant result of (I = -0.005, p = 0.421). However, rather than disproving the proposed Green Paradox, this highlights that ecological gentrification is not a uniform, city-wide phenomenon. Since boroughs like Kensington & Chelsea (historically wealthy, established parks) causing it to behave differently to Boroughs like Tower Hamlets or Newham (historically deprived, rapid recent green regeneration). And since these dynamics are so different, they cancel each other out when averaged into a single global statistic.

Therefore to address this, I employed Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA). While the global trend is flat, LISA calculates a Local Moran's I for every single LSOA individually, allowing us to strip away the global noise as a result and thus identify statistically significant (p < 0.05) spatial clusters.

This means that the resulting LISA Cluster Map reveals the following distinct spatial regimes:

  • High-High (Dark Red): Areas where high structural deprivation spatially coincideing with high green infrastructure density. These are the exact, quantifiable neighbourhoods most vulnerable to ecological displacement.
  • Low-Low (Light Blue): Low deprivation surrounded by low park density.
  • High-Low (Light Red) & Low-High (Dark Blue): These are Spatial outliers where the variables are inversely related.

By utilising libpysal (with a KNN weights matrix to avoid geometric "islands") and esda, this analysis suggests that the Green Paradox is a highly localised issue, pinpointing the exact East London communities facing the highest pressure from green gentrification.

In [20]:
park_map
Out[20]:
Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook

Interactive Map Description

The final interactive map is built in Folium (essentially a Python wrapper for Leaflet.js), designed to make the spatial distribution easy to explore at high levels of abstraction. The map is centred using fit_bounds of the returned park points given a borough, which provides an automatic, data-driven extent rather some hard-coded view. A clean, neutral light basemap (CartoDB positron) is used so that thematic content (parks) remains visually dominant, helping support readability and avoiding unnecessary colour competition with the data layer.

Each park returned by the OpenStreetMap Overpass API is rendered as a stylised CircleMarker with a consistent colour palette (dark green outline with lighter green fill) to signal the “greenness” without obscuring and conflicting with the basemap. Interactivity is implemented through both a tooltip (on hover) and a popup (on click), allowing users to identify specific sites quickly whilst keeping the display uncluttered.

A key design decision was the use of MarkerCluster (from folium.plugins). Without clustering, the map contained ~87 individual markers that would've overlapped heavily at city-wide zoom levels, thus outputting a messy “green dot cloud” that would've reduced interpretability and made the interaction frustrating. Clustering also improves user experience by aggregating nearby parks into a single symbol with a count, then progressively “unpacking” the data as the user zooms in. This supports both overview (how greenspace is distributed around a borough) and detail (where individual parks are), aligning with good web mapping practice/design for dense point layers.

Finally, LayerControl is included to make the map extensible (e.g adding deprivation or house-price overlays later), ensuring the interactive design can evolve into a coherent multi layer amalgamation.

The interactive map reveals a clear spatial pattern, directly portrays the Green Paradox , Tower of Hamlets stands out with 233 parks, the highest number in the list of boroughs. When the IMD chlorpleth layer is activated, a pattern emerges in a way such that the highest concentration of parks (Tower of Hamlets) coincides with areas of Higher deprivation , opposed to , lower deprivation boroughs such as Kensington included 32 parks

Therefore, out of the 534 total mapped parks (after spatial join), 412 (77.2%) fall within the 40% most deprived LSOAs. Tower Hamlets alone accounted for 229 (42.8%) of total parks but contained some of the highest IMD Scores.

Conclusion

This project investigates the "Green Paradox", the phenomenon where urban greening inadvertently drives up gentrification and displaces vulnerable populations. By employing geovisualisation tools, I first established the socio-economic baseline of London. Map 1 (Average House Price) & Map 2 (Average IMD Score) revealed a stark spatial "rent gap" in East London, highlighting historically deprived areas ripe for ecological gentrification. To dynamically explore the environmental interventions driving this shift, I developed an interactive, API-driven map using Folium & the OpenStreetMap Overpass API. These visualisations help bridge the gap between static socio-economic vulnerability and real-time green infrastructure, concluding a comprehensive spatial narrative of ecological exclusion.

Specifically, each component of this analysis directly constructs the narrative of the Green Paradox:

  • The Static Socio-Economic Maps (IMD & Prices) expose the baseline vulnerability, outlining the downturned property values and high deprivation required for the paradox to take place.
  • The Interactive Folium Map pinpoints the spatial distribution of green amenities (the catalyst to this conondrum), revealing a disproportionate concentration of parks in historically deprived boroughs like Tower Hamlets for example.
  • The Bivariate LISA Cluster Analysis mathematically implies the paradox by explicitly identifying the active "High-High" zones neighborhoods, where high deprivation directly intersects with high park density, marking ground zero for impending green gentrification.
